evidence.title: A Multicenter, Open-Label Continuation Trial Evaluating the Tolerability and Activity of Depsipeptide (FK228) in Patients That Have Completed a Prior Clinical Study With Depsipeptide.

evidence.title: Brief Summary
evidence.snippet: To evaluate the safety and tolerability of extended treatment with depsipeptide in patients who have at least demonstrated stable disease on prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trials, and in the opinion of their physician/ investigator might benefit from continued treatment with depsipeptide

evidence.title: Duvelisib plus romidepsin in relapsed/refractory T cell lymphomas: a phase 1b/2a trial.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Duvelisib plus romidepsin in relapsed/refractory T cell lymphomas: a phase 1b/2a trial.
Authors: Horwitz Steven M, Nirmal Ajit J, Rahman Jahan et al.
Journal: Nature medicine (2024)
Abstract: PI3K-δ inhibitors have shown impressive activity in lymphoid malignancies but have been hampered by autoimmune and infectious toxicities, leading to market withdrawals. We previously demonstrated activity of the PI3K-δγ inhibitor duvelisib in T cell lymphomas (TCLs) that was associated with inflamma

evidence.title: Depsipeptide (FK228) as a novel histone deacetylase inhibitor: mechanism of action and anticancer activity.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Depsipeptide (FK228) as a novel histone deacetylase inhibitor: mechanism of action and anticancer activity.
Authors: Lech-Maranda Ewa, Robak Ewa, Korycka Anna et al.
Journal: Mini reviews in medicinal chemistry (2007)
Abstract: Depsipeptide (FK228), a new histone deacetylase inhibitor, has been recently introduced into clinical trials. This agent shows interesting metabolic properties, novel mechanism of action, and is undergoing phase I-II clinical studies in hematopoietic malignancies and solid tumors. Mechanism of actio

evidence.title: Romidepsin Plus CHOP Versus CHOP in Patients With Previously Untreated Peripheral T-Cell Lymphoma: Results of the Ro-CHOP Phase III Study (Conducted by LYSA).
evidence.snippet:
Title: Romidepsin Plus CHOP Versus CHOP in Patients With Previously Untreated Peripheral T-Cell Lymphoma: Results of the Ro-CHOP Phase III Study (Conducted by LYSA).
Authors: Bachy Emmanuel, Camus Vincent, Thieblemont Catherine et al.
Journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology (2022)
Abstract: PURPOSE: Romidepsin, a histone deacetylase inhibitor, has demonstrated activity in relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL) as a single agent. Cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (CHOP) therapy is widely used as first-line treatment of PTCL; however, it has lim

evidence.title: Combined oral 5-azacytidine and romidepsin are highly effective in patients with PTCL: a multicenter phase 2 study.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Combined oral 5-azacytidine and romidepsin are highly effective in patients with PTCL: a multicenter phase 2 study.
Authors: Falchi Lorenzo, Ma Helen, Klein Sandra et al.
Journal: Blood (2021)
Abstract: Peripheral T-cell lymphomas (PTCLs) are uniquely vulnerable to epigenetic modifiers. We demonstrated in vitro synergism between histone deacetylase inhibitors and DNA methyltransferase inhibitors in preclinical models of T-cell lymphoma. In a phase 1 trial, we found oral 5-azacytidine and romidepsin

annotations.reasoning:
[Pass 1 extraction] Peptide Identity: Depsipeptide (FK228); confirmed as a peptide; molecular class is a depsipeptide.
Database Matches: No evidence found.
Mechanism: Works via non-antimicrobial mechanism (histone deacetylase inhibitor, anticancer activity).
Therapeutic Target: Peripheral T Cell Lymphoma (PTCL)
Immune Direction: Immune-neutral (anticancer/metabolic)
[Pass 2 decision] Classification: Other
Reasoning: Step 1 — The intervention is a peptide.  
Step 2 — This peptide is not an AMP because the mechanism of action is non-antimicrobial, specifically as a histone deacetylase inhibitor with anticancer activity. It does not directly kill or inhibit microorganisms through antimicrobial mechanisms.  
Step 3 — Not applicable since it is determined to be "Other" at Step 2.

[Pass 1 route extraction] Here are the extracted information:

1. Protocol Route:
Not specified in the ClinicalTrials.gov protocol description.

2. FDA/Label Route:
Not found in any of the provided sources.

3. Literature Route:
From PMID:16435156 (Konstantinopoulos et al., 2006):
"...FK228 was administered as a continuous intravenous infusion over 4 hours, at doses ranging from 10 to 25 mg/m2/day for up to 5 days..."
From
[Pass 2 classification] Delivery Mode: Injection/Infusion - IV
Evidence: PMID:16435156 (Konstantinopoulos et al., 2006)
Reasoning: The most specific route is intravenous (IV) injection over 4 hours, as stated in the literature source.

opinions.reasoning: Depsipeptide is explicitly named as the active drug being studied in the trial.

Peptides are typically defined as linear chains of amino acids linked by peptide bonds. The evidence provided does not contain any information that would suggest the drug in question, depsipeptide (FK228), has this structure or function. Instead, it indicates that it is a small molecule with specific biological activity related to histone deacetylase inhibition. Therefore, based on the available data, we cannot conclude that the primary intervention is a peptide therapeutic.
Depsipeptides are known to be peptides with additional functional groups attached, and the structural data provided clearly indicates that the drug in this trial is a depsipeptide.

fields.reconciler_reasoning: The term "depsipeptide" is explicitly used to describe the drug being studied in this clinical trial. While depsipeptides are typically considered peptides with additional functional groups, the primary evidence clearly identifies FK228 as a depsipeptide, which aligns with the definition provided by verifiers who agree that it falls under the peptide category despite its structural complexity. The conflicting opinion from verifier_2 does not provide sufficient counter-evidence to refute this cla

opinions.reasoning: While depsipeptide is a molecule that can inhibit histone deacetylases (HDACs), its mechanism of action does not directly kill or disrupt pathogens. It works by modulating gene expression within cells, influencing cellular processes like proliferation and differentiation. This trial focuses on the safety and tolerability of continued treatment in patients with PTCL, suggesting its primary aim is not to treat an active infection but rather to manage a pre-existing condition.

Dapsipeptide acts by inhibiting histone deacetylase activity which can lead to changes in gene expression but does not involve direct killing of pathogens. Therefore, it doesn't meet the criteria for being classified as an antimicrobial peptide (AMP) that physically disrupts or kills microorganisms through its own biochemical action. Hence, it falls under 'Other'.

While Depsipeptide is primarily used as an anticancer drug, its interaction with ClpP suggests it may also have antimicrobial properties. As it directly disrupts and kills microorganisms through its own biochemical action, it falls under the category of Antimicrobial Peptides (AMP). However, further research is needed to confirm its antimicrobial activity in infection settings.

fields.reconciler_reasoning: The evidence indicates that depsipeptide is primarily used as an anticancer drug, specifically for treating Peripheral T Cell Lymphoma. While there are some suggestions in the literature about its potential antimicrobial properties through interactions with ClpP (e.g., [pdbe] PDB:3mt6), these do not provide sufficient evidence to classify it definitively under AMP categories. identificationModule.briefTitle: A Multicenter, Open-Label Continuation Trial Evaluating the Tolerability and Activity of Depsipeptide (FK228) in Patients That Have Completed a Prior Clinical Study With Depsipeptide.

descriptionModule.briefSummary: To evaluate the safety and tolerability of extended treatment with depsipeptide in patients who have at least demonstrated stable disease on prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trials, and in the opinion of their physician/ investigator might benefit from continued treatment with depsipeptide

eligibilityModule.eligibilityCriteria:
Inclusion Criteria:

Written informed consent/ authorization Patient has completed 6 cycles of therapy in a prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trial Patient has immediate past participation (not to exceed 21 days from Day 15 of cycle 6 in the previous study) in a prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trial Patient has demonstrated stable disease, partial response or complete response as best overall response in their prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trial and such response must be ongoing at the time of enrollment Patient must have serum potassium levels \>4.0mEq/L and serum magnesium levels \>2.0mg/dL Negative urine or serum pregnancy test on females of childbearing potential Sexually active females of child-bearing potential must be willing to practice reliable methods of birth control to prevent pregnancy after entering the trial Sexually active males must be willing to practice reliable methods of birht control after entering the trial

Exclusion Criteria:

Patients wiht known cardiac abnormalities such as

* congenital long QT syndrome
* QTc interval \>480 milliseconds Patients with any cardiac arrhythmia requiring anti-arrhythmic medication Patients who have had a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) eg. angina Canadian class II-IV (see appendix G)\> In any patient whom there is doubt, the patient should have a stress imaging study and, if abnormal, angiopathy to define whether or not CAD is present Patients who have had a myocardial infarction within 12 months of study entry. Patients with an ECG recorded at screening showing evidence of cardiac ishemia (ST depression of \>2mm). If in any doubt, the patient should have a stress imaging study and if abnormal, angiograpphy to define whether or not CAD is present Patients with congestive heart failure that meets New York heart Association (NYHA) Class II to IV (see appendix F) definitions and/or ejection fraction \<40% by MUGA scan or \<50% by echocardiogram and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) Patients with a history of sustained VT,VF,Torsade de Pointes, or cardiac arrest unless currently addressed with an automatic implantable cardioverter defibrillator (AICD) Patients with a hypertrophic cardiomelagy or restrictive cardiomyopathy from prior treatment or other causes (in doubt, see ejection fraction criteria above) Patients with uncontrolled hypertension ie \> 160/95 Concomitant use of medications which may cause a prolongation of QT/QTc Concomitant use of medications that are inhibitors of the cytochrome P-450 isoenzyme CYP 3A4 Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) \< 1.5 X 109 cells/L Platelet count \<75 x 109 cells/L Serum creatinine concentration \>2 mg/dL or creatinine clearance \<40mL/min AST (aspartate aminotransferase ) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) \>2.0 x ULN or \>5 X ULN in presence of demonstable liver metastasis Bilirubin concentration \> 1.25 x ULN or \> 2.0 x ULN in presence of demonstrable liver metastases Serum potassium \<4.0mEq/L and serum magnesium \<2.0 mg/dL Failure to recover of any drug-related non-hematological toxicity to grade 1 oe less or to baseline values unless otherwise indicated Concomitant use of warfarin (due to a potentialdrug to drug interaction with depsipeptide) Patient is pregnant or nursing Patient had been on prior Gloucester-sponsored depsipeptide clinical trial, left the trial and then received alternative neoplastic therapy

Abstract: PURPOSE: The fundamental role of epigenetic events in carcinogenesis has resulted in the evolution of epigenetic targeting as a new paradigm in anticancer therapeutics. Aberrant histone deacetylase (HDAC) activity has been documented in many human malignancies resulting in the repression of tumor su

citations.title: Duvelisib plus romidepsin in relapsed/refractory T cell lymphomas: a phase 1b/2a trial.
citations.snippet:
Title: Duvelisib plus romidepsin in relapsed/refractory T cell lymphomas: a phase 1b/2a trial.
Authors: Horwitz Steven M, Nirmal Ajit J, Rahman Jahan et al.
Journal: Nature medicine (2024)
Abstract: PI3K-δ inhibitors have shown impressive activity in lymphoid malignancies but have been hampered by autoimmune and infectious toxicities, leading to market withdrawals. We previously demonstrated activity of the PI3K-δγ inhibitor duvelisib in T cell lymphomas (TCLs) that was associated with inflamma

Abstract: Depsipeptide (FK228), a new histone deacetylase inhibitor, has been recently introduced into clinical trials. This agent shows interesting metabolic properties, novel mechanism of action, and is undergoing phase I-II clinical studies in hematopoietic malignancies and solid tumors. Mechanism of actio

Abstract: PURPOSE: Romidepsin, a histone deacetylase inhibitor, has demonstrated activity in relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL) as a single agent. citations.title: Combined oral 5-azacytidine and romidepsin are highly effective in patients with PTCL: a multicenter phase 2 study.

Title: Combined oral 5-azacytidine and romidepsin are highly effective in patients with PTCL: a multicenter phase 2 study.

Journal: Blood (2021)
Abstract: Peripheral T-cell lymphomas (PTCLs) are uniquely vulnerable to epigenetic modifiers. We demonstrated in vitro synergism between histone deacetylase inhibitors and DNA methyltransferase inhibitors in preclinical models of T-cell lymphoma.
